title: Dog Bite Injuries
description: California is a strict liability state for dog bites — meaning the dog owner is liable regardless of whether the dog had previously shown aggression. Arizona has similar protections. If you were bitten or attacked by a dog, prompt medical care and documentation are critical.
metaTitle: Dog Bite Injury Guide — Strict Liability Laws in CA & AZ
metaDescription: What to do after a dog bite in California or Arizona. Strict liability laws, evidence checklist, medical documentation, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Lack of Owner Control
commonCauses.description: Dogs allowed to roam without a leash, or left unattended in public spaces, pose a direct risk to others.

commonCauses.title: Failure to Warn Visitors
commonCauses.description: Property owners who do not warn guests about an aggressive or unpredictable dog may bear additional liability.

commonCauses.title: Provocation (Disputed)
commonCauses.description: Owners sometimes claim the victim provoked the dog. California and Arizona strict liability applies regardless of provocation in most cases, though this is a common defense argument.

commonCauses.title: Escaped or Unsecured Dogs
commonCauses.description: Dogs that escape fenced yards or break free from restraints and bite passersby on public property.

commonCauses.title: Delivery and Service Workers
commonCauses.description: Mail carriers, package delivery workers, and utility workers face elevated dog bite risk due to regular home access.

commonCauses.title: Children Left Unattended with Dogs
commonCauses.description: Children are disproportionately the victims of serious dog bites, often from dogs known to the family.

immediateSteps.title: Seek medical care immediately
immediateSteps.description: Dog bites carry high infection risk, including rabies. Wash the wound thoroughly and go to urgent care or an ER the same day, even if the wound appears minor.

immediateSteps.title: Identify the dog and its owner
immediateSteps.description: Get the owner's name, address, and phone number. Ask for proof of rabies vaccination. If the owner is unknown, report to animal control.

immediateSteps.title: Report the bite to animal control
immediateSteps.description: File a report with your local animal control agency. This creates an official record and triggers a quarantine or observation period for the dog.

immediateSteps.title: Photograph the wound before and after treatment
immediateSteps.description: Document the severity of the injury with photos taken at the scene and throughout your recovery.

immediateSteps.title: Get witness information
immediateSteps.description: Names and contact details from anyone who saw the attack.

immediateSteps.title: Photograph the location
immediateSteps.description: Document where the bite occurred — public sidewalk, park, the owner's property — along with any fencing, signage, or leash conditions.

immediateSteps.title: Keep all medical records and bills
immediateSteps.description: Document every medical visit related to the bite, including follow-up wound care, rabies prophylaxis, and any psychological treatment.

immediateSteps.title: Document emotional impact
immediateSteps.description: Dog bites can cause lasting psychological distress, especially in children. Keep a journal of any anxiety, sleep disruption, or fear of dogs.

items: Owner's name, address, and phone number
Proof of rabies vaccination (request from owner)
Photos of wounds immediately after the bite
Photos of wounds throughout recovery (daily if significant)
Emergency room and follow-up visit records
Records of rabies prophylaxis or any vaccinations administered
Records of any surgical treatment or wound care
Photos of the location where the attack occurred
Photos of any fencing, gate, or restraint that failed
Names and contact info of any witnesses
All medical bills related to the bite
Costs of psychological or counseling treatment
Any prior animal control reports involving the same dog
Neighbor or witness accounts of the dog's prior aggressive behavior

timelineRisks.action: Wash the wound and seek medical care the same day. If the dog's rabies status is unknown, post-exposure prophylaxis must begin promptly.

timelineRisks.risk: Animal control report must be filed
timelineRisks.action: File an animal control report promptly to trigger dog quarantine. This also creates an official record of the incident.
Most dog bite claims go through the dog owner's homeowner or renter insurance. The insurer will investigate.
If the bite occurred on government property or involved a government-owned animal, shorter claims deadlines apply.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for dog bite personal injury claims
timelineRisks.action: California has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims, including dog bites.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for dog bite claims
timelineRisks.action: Arizona has a 1-year statute of limitations specifically for dog bite claims under its strict liability statute. Consult an attorney promptly.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Most dog bite claims are paid through the dog owner's homeowner or renter insurance liability coverage. Many policies cover $100,000-$300,000 in liability.
Some homeowner policies exclude certain breeds (pit bulls, Rottweilers) from coverage. If the dog owner's breed is excluded, recovering compensation may require alternative approaches.
Under California Civil Code 3342, dog owners are strictly liable for bites that occur in public places or lawfully in private places — regardless of the dog's prior history.
Arizona has a similar strict liability dog bite statute. The owner is liable if the bitten person was in a public place or lawfully in a private place.
While strict liability applies broadly, owners may argue the victim provoked the dog. This is frequently raised and requires factual response.

whenToGetLawyer: Your injuries required medical treatment beyond basic first aid
The wound required stitches, surgery, or resulted in scarring
The dog owner's insurer denied coverage or disputed liability
The bite has caused lasting psychological impact or fear
The bite occurred to a child
The dog had prior incidents of aggression
You are unsure how to navigate the homeowner insurance claim
